facts.label: THE PRINCE WHO STOPPED PLAY
facts.story: With France leading 3-1 late in their group match, a slick move finished by Alain Giresse landed the ball in Kuwait's net. Kuwait's players stopped defending mid-play, insisting they'd heard a whistle from the stands. As the referee let the goal stand, Sheikh Fahad Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah, the Emir's brother and head of Kuwait's football federation, walked out of the stands and onto the pitch to argue the case himself. The referee reversed the goal. Kuwait still lost 4-1, and the referee was later banned for life for allowing the interference.

facts.label: A SCREAMER TO OPEN THE ACCOUNT
facts.story: Kuwait's traditional colors, blue for home and white for away, had never been seen at a World Cup before their debut in Spain. In their first-ever match, trailing Czechoslovakia to a first-half penalty, Faisal Al-Dakhil rose to head home a corner in the 57th minute, Kuwait's first World Cup goal, to salvage a stunning 1-1 draw against a team that had reached the previous tournament's second round.

annotations.label: AN OPENING-DAY DRAW
annotations.story: Faisal Al-Dakhil headed home a corner in the 57th minute against Czechoslovakia, Kuwait's first-ever World Cup goal, canceling out an early Panenka penalty to secure a 1-1 draw in their tournament debut.

annotations.label: A SHEIKH INTERVENES
annotations.story: Trailing France 3-1, Kuwait's players stopped play believing they'd heard a whistle from the stands when France scored a fourth. Sheikh Fahad Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah walked onto the pitch to protest, and the referee disallowed the goal before France closed out a 4-1 win regardless.
